title: Max-Cut on a 4-Node Graph
summary: The smallest interesting Max-Cut. 4 nodes, 4 qubits — comfortably fits any real superconducting QPU.
problem_statement: I have a graph with 4 nodes labeled 0, 1, 2, 3 and edges: (0,1), (0,2), (1,3), (2,3). Partition the nodes into two groups so the number of edges crossing between the groups is maximized. Tell me which nodes go in each group.
real_world_example: The 4-node cycle is the textbook 'smallest interesting graph problem' — big enough for a nontrivial partition, small enough to fit a demo without slack qubits. Same shape appears in tiny routing decisions and 2×2 assignment scoring.

expected_pattern.variables: x_i in {0, 1} — which group node i belongs to
expected_pattern.objective: maximize sum over edges (i,j) of (x_i + x_j - 2*x_i*x_j)

expected_solution_summary: Bipartition {0, 3} vs {1, 2} cuts all 4 edges. Symmetric with the swap.
learning_notes: The natural unconstrained binary encoding lowers to exactly 4 qubits with no slack. This is the recommended smoke test for the real-QPU path: click Solve with Wukong picked and you should see the same energy from qaoa_originqc and all classical solvers.
